chapter_summary: The opening chapter of the Gheranda Samhita, framed as a dialogue: the seeker Canda Kapali approaches the sage Gheranda and asks to be taught Ghatastha Yoga (the yoga of the body-vessel) as the cause of knowledge of reality (tattvajnana). Gheranda praises the question and declares that there is no bondage like maya, no power greater than yoga, no friend higher than knowledge, and no enemy worse than ego. He explains that the jiva, like a potter's wheel turning by the force of past good and bad deeds (karma), revolves through birth and death; the body is an unbaked clay pot that must be fired in the flame of yoga to be purified. He then lays out the sevenfold discipline of Ghatastha Yoga — purification, firmness, steadiness, patience, lightness, direct perception, and non-attachment — mapped onto seven limbs (shatkarma, asana, mudra, pratyahara, pranayama, dhyana, samadhi) culminating in liberation. The chapter then begins the shatkarmas, detailing the four kinds of dhauti (cleansing), especially the fourfold antardhauti (vatasara air-cleansing, varisara water-cleansing, vahnisara fire/navel-cleansing, and bahishkrita), promising that mastery yields a "divine body" (devadeha) and freedom from disease — secret practices not to be revealed.

story.title: The Potter's Wheel and the Unbaked Pot: Gheranda Teaches Canda Kapali
story.arc: A seeker named Canda Kapali travels to the hut of the sage Gheranda, bows in humility and devotion, and asks for the teaching of Ghatastha Yoga — the yoga of the body-vessel — as the gateway to knowledge of reality. Gheranda, delighted, praises the question and opens with the deepest diagnosis: nothing binds like maya, nothing liberates like yoga, knowledge is the only true friend and ego the only true enemy. He gives the central image: the embodied soul is spun helplessly on the potter's wheel of its own good and bad karma, turning endlessly through birth and death; the body itself is an unbaked clay pot soaking in water, perpetually decaying — useless until it is fired hard in the flame of yoga. From this image he derives the whole path: a sevenfold discipline (purification, firmness, steadiness, patience, lightness, direct perception, non-attachment) realized through seven limbs (shatkarma, asana, mudra, pratyahara, pranayama, dhyana, samadhi) that ends without doubt in liberation. He then begins the first limb, the shatkarmas, descending into the concrete craft of purification — the four dhautis, and the four internal cleansings of air, water, fire, and expulsion — each described with the secrecy of a guarded mystery, each promising that the perfected practitioner sheds the mortal 'pot of disease' and attains a divine, godlike body.
story.timeline_note: Single uninterrupted teaching session ('ekadā' — 'once'); the frame is a one-time dialogue with no internal time jumps. Opens with an invocatory salutation (GhS_1.0) before the narrative 'once, Canda Kapali went...' (GhS_1.1). The text shown ends mid-flow within the dhauti section (GhS_1.22), so this is the first installment of a continuing instruction.
story.comic_potential: The deadpan understatement of yogic cleansing instructions — 'drink air slowly like a crow's beak, churn the belly, expel it downward' — described with the gravity of cosmic liberation. The earnest secrecy ('this is supremely secret, never to be revealed') wrapped around what is, plainly, an elaborate internal plumbing routine. And the image of the soul as a hapless clay pot spun on a potter's wheel by a cow-driven gear is begging for an animated visual gag.

teachings.teaching: There is no bondage equal to maya, no power greater than yoga, no friend higher than knowledge (jnana), and no enemy worse than ego (ahamkara).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya framework this is the entire map in one verse: ahamkara — the false 'I' rooted in the lower centers — is the single knot that maya tightens, and the only real adversary on the path. Yoga is named 'the greatest power' because, unlike philosophy, it is a *kriya* — an applied technology that actually severs the knot rather than describing it. Knowledge (jnana) here is not bookish information but the direct experiential gnosis (tattvajnana) that dawns when prana is mastered; it is the 'friend' because it is what survives when the ego-pot is finally fired away.

teachings.teaching: The embodied soul (jiva) revolves through birth and death by the force of its karma, exactly as a potter's wheel is spun up and down by the ox that drives it; good and bad deeds together produce the body, and the body in turn generates fresh karma.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma reads the potter's-wheel (ghatayantra) image as the mechanics of the karmic vritti-cycle: as long as prana flows outward and downward through ordinary breathing, the wheel keeps turning and the jiva is passive cargo on it. Kriya's purpose is to redirect that very pranic force inward and upward (the reversal of the 'wheel') so the soul stops being spun and becomes the still axle — the witness at the center. The body is not condemned; it is precisely the instrument through which the cycle is broken, because the wheel can only be stopped from within the apparatus itself.

teachings.teaching: The body is an unbaked clay pot soaking in water, always decaying; it must be fired and hardened in the flame of yoga (yogagni) to achieve the purification of the vessel (ghata-shuddhi).
teachings.lens_note: This is the literal charter of Kriya as Sharma teaches it: the 'fire of yoga' (yogānala) is the awakened inner fire — the heat generated when prana is concentrated and the jatharagni / kundalini-fire is kindled by sustained practice. An unbaked pot dissolves back into mud (re-death); a fired pot holds its form and can hold the nectar. Sharma insists the body must be deliberately *cooked* by disciplined kriya before any higher state is stable — purification is not a metaphor for moral effort but a real physiological transmutation that makes the body a fit, non-leaking container for consciousness (the 'devadeha').

teachings.teaching: Ghatastha Yoga is a sevenfold discipline: purification (shodhana), firmness (dridhata), steadiness (sthairya), patience (dhairya), lightness (laghava), direct perception (pratyaksha) and non-attachment (nirlipta) — attained respectively through shatkarma, asana, mudra, pratyahara, pranayama, dhyana, and samadhi — and it culminates beyond doubt in liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma treats this seven-rung ladder as the engineered sequence Kriya follows in practice, not an arbitrary list: you cannot leap to dhyana while the vessel still leaks. Purification (shatkarma) clears the channels; asana stabilizes the body so prana can settle; mudra and pranayama work the energy directly; only on that prepared ground do dhyana (which yields *pratyaksha* — direct seeing of the Atman, not belief) and samadhi (which yields *nirlipta* — the unsmeared, witness state) become possible. The promise 'muktir eva na saṃśayaḥ' — liberation without doubt — is Sharma's recurring claim that yoga is an exact science with a guaranteed result when the steps are honored in order.

teachings.teaching: Through the dhauti cleansings — especially the fourfold internal antardhauti of air (vatasara), water (varisara), fire/navel (vahnisara) and expulsion (bahishkrita) — the body is made spotless, the gastric fire is increased, all diseases are destroyed, and one who perfects them attains a divine body; these practices are supremely secret and must never be casually revealed.
teachings.lens_note: Here the lens and the source agree completely with Sharma's lived emphasis: the shatkarmas are not hygiene for its own sake but the kindling of the inner fire (dehānala / jatharagni) that the whole edifice of Kriya depends on — a strong inner fire is what burns the karmic residue and dries out the 'soaking pot.' The insistence on secrecy ('parā gopyā, na prakāśyā kadācana') is the textual anchor for the live system's Practice-and-Initiation guardrail: these are not self-study techniques but transmissions that belong to the direct Guru–disciple relationship. The 'devadeha' (divine body) is Sharma's goal of a transmuted, deathless-disposed physical vehicle — the fired pot that can finally hold the divine.

chapter_summary: Gheranda Samhita GhS_1.22 to GhS_1.44 continue the shatkarma cleansings of ghatastha yoga. It completes the secret prakshalana of washing the abdominal shakti nadi navel deep in water, said unattainable even by the gods and to grant a divine body, devadeha. It gives Danta dhauti, cleansing teeth root, lengthening the lambika by milking with butter, cleansing the ear cavities to awaken nada, and pressing the frontal aperture so divine sight, divyadrishti, arises. It gives Hrd dhauti in three modes, Danda reed stick, Vamana water vomiting, Vasa cloth swallowing, against heart disease and kapha pitta. Finally Mula shodhana cleanses the root, easing apana and kindling the digestive fire, vahni mandala.

story.title: The Vessel Made Divine, Gherandas Secret Cleansings
story.arc: Gheranda continues the shatkarma inward and downward through the body vessel. He reveals the secret washing of the abdominal shakti nadi navel deep, so guarded the gods cannot reach it, yet granting a divine body. He cleanses the head gates, the teeth root, the tongue milked until it lengthens, the ear cavities until the inner sound dawns, and the frontal aperture until divine sight is born. He cleanses the heart by stick, vomited water, and cloth, then the root, kindling the digestive fire so the body shines.

teachings.teaching: The shakti nadi prakshalana, washed navel deep in water, is supremely secret, para gopya, unattainable even by the gods, and by mere cleansing the body becomes a divine body, devadeha.
teachings.lens_note: For Sharma the secrecy is transmission, the deepest pranic purification comes only by the guru given practice working the shakti nadi through which kundalini rises, and devadeha is literal, the vessel transmuted toward the deathless light body.

teachings.teaching: Maha dhauti does not arise in one who has not first mastered the ardha retention capacity, purification follows a strict order of prerequisites.
teachings.lens_note: Sharmas krama, no opening comes by skipping steps, an unprepared vessel yields nothing.

teachings.teaching: Danta dhauti cleanses five seats, teeth root, tongue root, the two ear cavities and the frontal aperture, supreme because these serve dharana.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma reads the five head gates as apertures of dharana, cleansed because a fouled gate cannot hold prana, the teeth and tongue root bordering khechari territory.

teachings.teaching: Milking with butter and lengthening the lambika removes kapha and conquers ageing, death and disease, the tongue becoming dirgha lambika.
teachings.lens_note: The physical groundwork of khechari mudra, the tongue lengthened to seal the cranial aperture and reach the nectar amrita, the closed gate stopping it being burned by the gastric fire so decay halts.

teachings.teaching: Rubbing the ear cavities makes inner sound, nada, manifest, and pressing the frontal aperture clears kapha so the nadi purifies and divine sight, divyadrishti, arises.
teachings.lens_note: For Sharma the doorways of laya yoga, anahata nada and ajna divyadrishti, a gross cleansing precipitating subtle perception, physical shuddhi the precondition for the divine senses.

teachings.teaching: Hrd dhauti is threefold, by danda reed stick, by vamana vomiting, by vasa cloth, and destroys heart disease, gulma, fever, spleen, leprosy and kapha pitta.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma reads heart cleansing as clearing the anahata seat where prana stagnates, removing kapha pitta lifting the doshic veil that blocks prana from rising past the heart.

teachings.teaching: Until the root, mula, is cleansed, apana stays harsh, apana krurata, so root cleansing softens the bowels, cures indigestion, and kindles the digestive fire.
teachings.lens_note: The lens keystone, the ascent depends on apana at muladhara reversed and merged with prana to awaken the fire that lifts shakti, kindling the vahni mandala the first cause of the upward process, nothing rises until the bottom is clean.

chapter_summary: The closing portion of the Gheranda Samhita's first upadesha (instruction) on the shatkarma — the six purification practices of ghatastha-yoga (yoga of the body-vessel) — taught by sage Gheranda to his disciple Chanda(kapali). It completes the description of basti (yogic enema, in jala/water and shushka/dry forms, done in utkatasana with ashwini-mudra to draw and expel, curing constipation, indigestion, urinary disorders, gudavarta and disorders of krura-vayu), neti (threading a fine thread through the nostril and out the mouth, granting khechari-siddhi, divya-drishti/divine sight, and destroying kapha-dosha), lauliki/nauli (churning the belly rapidly side to side to destroy all disease and kindle the body's fire), trataka (steady unblinking gaze at a subtle point until tears flow, yielding the shambhavi state and divya-drishti), and kapalabhati in its three modes — vamakrama, vyutkrama, and shitkrama (breath and water drawn through ida/pingala and nostrils/mouth to destroy kapha-dosha, ward off old age and fever, and make the body free and luminous "like Kamadeva"). It closes the first upadesha (the colophon naming shatkarma-sadhana) and opens the second upadesha on asana: Shiva long ago declared 84 lakh asanas matching the 84 lakh species of living beings, of which 84 are distinguished, 32 are auspicious and siddhi-giving in the mortal world; these 32 are enumerated (siddha, padma, bhadra, mukta, vajra, svastika, simha, gomukha, vira, dhanura, mrita, gupta, matsya, matsyendra, goraksha, paschimottana, utkata, sankata, mayura, kukkuta, kurma, uttanakurmaka, uttanamanduka, vriksha, manduka, garuda, vrisha, shalabha, makara, ushtra, bhujanga, yoga). The fragment ends mid-description of siddhasana (pressing the perineum, gazing between the brows with restrained senses).

story.title: Gheranda Purifies the Vessel: From the Six Cleansings to the Seat of the Adept
story.arc: Sage Gheranda, answering his disciple Chanda's wish to know the yoga that liberates, finishes describing the six purifications of ghatastha-yoga — the yoga of the body treated as a clay vessel that must be fired clean before it can hold the divine. He details basti (cleansing the lower gate through water and air drawn by ashwini-mudra), neti (threading the nasal passage to open divine sight), lauliki (churning the belly to wake its inner fire), trataka (the unblinking gaze that melts into the shambhavi state), and kapalabhati in three modes that scour away phlegm, age, and fever until the body becomes 'free-willed' and radiant 'like Kamadeva.' With the first instruction sealed by its colophon, Gheranda turns at once to the second teaching: invoking Shiva's ancient word that the 84 lakh asanas mirror the 84 lakh living forms, he narrows them to 32 perfected seats fit for mortals, names each one, and begins the first — siddhasana, the adept's seat, pressing the root and gazing between the brows — leaving the disciple poised at the threshold of stillness.

teachings.teaching: The body is a vessel (ghata) that must first be purified through the six shatkarma before any higher yoga can succeed; cleansing the gross channels removes the doshas (kapha, ama, krura-vayu) that obstruct the inner fire.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya framework the shatkarma are not hygiene but pranic preparation: the nadis must be physically scoured so that prana ceases to leak into disease and can instead be gathered and turned inward. Purifying the 'vessel' is the literal precondition for the prana to rise — Kriya begins only in a body where the channels no longer dissipate force into koshtha-dosha and ama-vata.

teachings.teaching: Basti, done in water and on land with ashwini-mudra (contracting and releasing the anus), cures constipation, urinary disorders and disorders of krura-vayu, making the body free-willed (svacchanda-deha) and like Kamadeva.
teachings.lens_note: The ashwini contraction at the lowest gate is mula-territory — the apana-vayu that normally flows downward and outward is reversed and lifted. For Sharma this downward-to-upward turning of apana is the seed gesture of all Kriya: the same root-lock that here merely cleanses the bowel is, refined, the lever that unites apana with prana at the navel and begins the upward kundalini movement.

teachings.teaching: Neti — drawing a fine thread through the nostril and out the mouth — perfects khechari-siddhi, destroys kapha-dosha, and gives divine sight (divya-drishti).
teachings.lens_note: That a nasal cleansing is said to yield khechari and divine sight signals, in Sharma's reading, that the practice opens the upper passage behind the palate — the gateway khechari-mudra later inhabits. The 'divine sight' is not metaphor but the awakening of the ajna region; the gross thread prepares the subtle path the tongue and gaze will later travel inward.

teachings.teaching: Lauliki/nauli — churning the belly rapidly to both sides — destroys all diseases and increases the body's fire (dehanala).
teachings.lens_note: Churning the navel directly stokes the agni at the manipura/navel center. In Sharma's Kriya the navel is where apana and prana are made to meet and ignite; lauliki is the gross mechanical rehearsal of that union, kindling the jatharagni whose subtler form is the inner heat that drives prana up the sushumna.

teachings.teaching: Trataka — gazing without blinking at a subtle point until tears fall — surely produces the shambhavi state, prevents eye disease and bestows divine sight.
teachings.lens_note: Trataka is, for Sharma, the explicit bridge from a gross cleansing kriya to dhyana: the fixed outer gaze collapses into shambhavi-mudra, the eyes turned to the point between the brows while awareness rests inward. It is where shatkarma quietly becomes meditation — the same one-pointedness that the gaze trains externally is what holds attention at the ajna in Kriya practice.

teachings.teaching: Kapalabhati in three modes — vamakrama, vyutkrama, shitkrama — moves breath and water through ida and pingala and the nostrils and mouth to destroy kapha-dosha, banish old age and fever, and make the body radiant like Kamadeva.
teachings.lens_note: Working alternately through ida (candra, the lunar/left channel) and pingala (the solar/right channel) is overt nadi-balancing: in Sharma's framework prana and apana, sun and moon, must be equalized in the side channels before either can enter sushumna. Kapalabhati's deliberate alternation of the two is the cleansing that precedes their merger — and the promised freedom from age and fever is the early sign of prana no longer leaking outward into the body's decay.

teachings.teaching: Shiva long ago declared 84 lakh asanas equal to the 84 lakh species of living beings; of these 84 are special, 32 are auspicious and confer siddhi in the mortal world — beginning with siddhasana, in which one presses the perineum, restrains the senses, and gazes between the brows.
teachings.lens_note: That the asanas number exactly the species of life encodes, for Sharma, that posture is the means of moving through and transcending every embodied form — the seat fixes the body so consciousness can climb beyond the form it sits in. Siddhasana being named first and described with perineal pressure plus the inter-brow gaze is no accident: it locks the lower gate (apana) and fixes attention at the ajna simultaneously — the complete static frame in which Kriya pranayama is performed. The 'siddha's seat' is literally the posture of the accomplished one because it is the one that makes the inner ascent possible.

chapter_summary: This section of the Gheranda Samhita (chapter 2 verses, GhS_2.7–2.27) is the asana (posture) catalogue of Gheranda's seven-limbed (saptanga) Hatha-Yoga, taught by sage Gheranda to king Chanda-Kapali. It defines a sequence of seated and reclining postures by exact anatomical construction and states the specific fruit of each: Siddhasana — heel pressed below the genitals, chin on the chest, gaze fixed between the eyebrows, declared the posture that 'breaks open the door-leaf of liberation' (mokshakapata-bhedana); Padmasana — feet crossed onto the opposite thighs, hands gripping the toes from behind, chin to chest, gaze on the nose-tip, destroyer of disease and disorder; Bhadrasana — soles joined below the perineum, hands holding the feet behind, with jalandhara (throat-lock) and nose-tip gaze, destroyer of all disease; Muktasana, Vajrasana, Svastikasana, Simhasana (with open mouth, throat-lock and nose-tip gaze, destroyer of all disease), Gomukhasana, Virasana, Dhanurasana (the body bent like a bow), Mritasana/Shavasana (the corpse pose that removes fatigue and rests the mind), Guptasana, Matsyasana (curing disease), Matsyendrasana, Gorakshasana (with throat-contraction and nose-tip gaze, granter of siddhi), Paschimottanasana / Yogendra-pitha, and Utkatasana. The passage repeatedly couples a precise physical lock (chin/throat/genital seat) with a fixed inward gaze and a named result — health, freedom from disorder, or attainment (siddhi).

story.title: The Sage's Catalogue of Seats: From the Corpse to the Door of Liberation
story.arc: Sage Gheranda lays out a graded gallery of bodily seats, each a precise lock of limb, throat and gaze. The arc moves from the most charged — Siddhasana, whose heel at the root and gaze between the brows 'breaks the door-leaf of liberation' — through a long body of postures that promise the lesser but indispensable gifts of health and freedom from disorder (Padmasana, Bhadrasana, Simhasana, Matsyasana), past the great equalizer Shavasana where the yogi lies as a corpse to still the mind, and on to the seats that grant siddhi (Vajrasana, Muktasana, Gorakshasana). The hidden through-line: every posture is built so the body becomes a stable, sealed vessel and the attention is bent inward to a single point — nose-tip or brow-center. The chapter is less a fitness manual than an instruction in how to make the flesh hold still long enough for the gaze to turn around.

teachings.teaching: Siddhasana is singled out above all: heel pressed at the perineum, chin locked to the heart, body made motionless (sthanu), gaze steady between the eyebrows — and this alone is named the posture that breaks open the door-leaf of liberation (mokshakapata-bhedana).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga lens this is the literal architecture of the practice, not metaphor. The heel sealing the root (muladhara) closes the lower gate so prana cannot leak downward; the chin-lock (jalandhara) closes the upper gate; with both gates sealed the energy has only one road — upward through sushumna. The gaze fixed between the brows is attention parked at ajna, the doorway. 'Breaking the door-leaf of moksha' is exact: the posture mechanically converts the body into a closed circuit so the awakened force can split the bolt of the cranial door. Sharma teaches that liberation is not believed into being but engineered by sealing the body and turning the gaze around.

teachings.teaching: Almost every posture pairs a physical lock with a single fixed gaze point — nose-tip (nasagra) in Padmasana, Bhadrasana, Simhasana, Gorakshasana, or brow-center (bhru-madhya) in Siddhasana and Matsyendrasana.
teachings.lens_note: For Sharma the drishti is the operative instruction; the limbs only hold the body still so the gaze can do its work. A wandering eye drags the mind; a fixed eye fixes the mind. Pinning attention to nose-tip or brow-center is the first deliberate withdrawal of awareness from the outgoing senses inward toward the spinal channel. The asana is scaffolding; the steady gaze is where the actual yoga begins — the body is locked precisely so the eye, and therefore the mind, can stop moving.

teachings.teaching: Most postures are credited with curing disease and disorder — Padmasana destroys vyadhi-vikara, Bhadrasana and Simhasana destroy all disease, Matsyasana removes disease, Shavasana removes fatigue and rests the mind.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma reads health here not as the goal but as the precondition. A diseased, restless body cannot sustain the long stillness the higher kriya demands; so the system first hands the seeker postures that make the instrument sound. The 'destruction of disease' is the clearing of obstacles (the body is the first obstacle), and Shavasana — lying like a corpse to still the chitta — is the bridge: only a body that can rest completely can be entered consciously. The therapeutic fruit is real but instrumental; it stabilizes the vehicle so prana can later be driven.

teachings.teaching: Certain seats are named specifically as granters of siddhi for the yogi — Muktasana is siddhi-giving, Vajrasana grants the yogi siddhi, Gorakshasana is declared a cause of attainment.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's framework siddhi is not stage magic but the matured result of the sealed body holding steady prana long enough for the inner process to ripen. The thunderbolt seat (Vajrasana) and the cowherd-king seat (Gorakshasana, named for Gorakshanath of the Nath line Sharma's own lineage honors) are flagged because their geometry — feet at the root, throat contracted, gaze inward — most efficiently fuses the downward and upward currents. The chapter quietly grades its postures: some merely heal, but a few are built to consummate. Sharma's caution applies — these fruits ripen only inside the living Guru-disciple bond; the verse names the seat, not the secret turning of force that the seat is built to hold.
